title: Jevons, Not Keynes: Why Lower Costs Expand Demand
body_html:
The Keynesian fear says lower prices cause hesitation. The Jevons-style reality often says lower costs create new use cases and expand total demand. The essay sides with the latter because history overwhelmingly does too. When something becomes cheaper, it does not merely become the same thing at a discount. It often becomes a platform for entirely new behavior.

That is why cheaper steel, energy, transport, computation, and communications ended up increasing total usage rather than suppressing it. Once costs fall, businesses can do things that were previously uneconomic. Consumers can buy what used to be luxuries. Complementary industries emerge. Whole demand curves change shape because the object is no longer competing in the same problem space.

This perspective matters for deflation because it reframes lower prices as market expansion rather than market decay. A lower price is not only relief for the buyer. It is a signal that the producer has widened the range of people or firms for whom the good now makes sense.

The result is that deflationary progress can enlarge the economy’s scope even while individual prices fall. The number goes down, and the civilization built on top of it gets bigger.
